name: Second Congo War (Africa's World War)
summary: The Second Congo War began in August 1998 after Laurent-Désiré Kabila broke with his Rwandan and Ugandan backers, leading to a regional war fought mainly on Congolese territory. The main war ended politically with the 2003 transition, but violence in Ituri and the Kivus continued. Directly documented violent deaths are in the tens of thousands, but excess-mortality studies attribute hundreds of thousands to several million deaths to disease, hunger, displacement and health-system collapse caused by the war.

sides.name: Government of the DRC / Kabila coalition

military_killed.notes: No reliable full military-death total by side was found. The DRC claimed 2,000 FAC deaths in fighting with Uganda or Uganda-supported rebels in its ICJ reparations case, but this is partial and litigation-specific. UCDP/PRIO gives 6,494 armed-actor deaths for the whole war but not by actor.

breakdown.cause: summary execution, mob killing, burning, shooting
breakdown.victims_group: Tutsi, Rwandan-origin civilians and suspected infiltrators in Kinshasa
breakdown.notes: At least 80 killed in late August 1998; broader campaign included several thousand arrests and several hundred disappearances, mostly summary executions, but not enough precision for aggregation.
More than 70 bodies were exhumed from Camp Marin after FAC executions.
Several sources cited by the Mapping Report indicate over 100 Tutsis died in Vyura in August 1998.
FAC troops aided by residents shot around 40 members of the Tutsi community.

breakdown.victims_group: young Tutsi or Rwandan military recruits at Kamina
breakdown.notes: Victims were unarmed; the total remains unclear but is thought to be at least 100.

breakdown.victims_group: Tutsi/Rwandan-origin civilians in Lubumbashi, Kipushi, Likasi and Kolwezi
breakdown.notes: ASADHO suggested over 70 victims; Mapping Report states several tens were executed.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The low estimate is a non-exhaustive sum of quantified examples only; the true total attributable to this coalition is unknown and likely higher.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Congolese civilians in government-held and contested areas
deaths_from_actions.notes: Government actions, alliances and detention practices contributed to insecurity and excess mortality, but no defensible side-specific indirect-death estimate was found.

military_killed.notes: No reliable full military-death total by side was found. UCDP/PRIO found 23 events involving Ugandan troops with 211 total deaths and only 3 reported Ugandan soldiers, but this is not a full UPDF/RPA/RCD/MLC count.

breakdown.victims_group: civilians in Kasika, Kilungutwe and Kalama
breakdown.notes: ANC/APR soldiers massacred over 1,000 civilians on 24 August 1998.

breakdown.victims_group: civilians in Makobola II, Bangwe, Katuta, Mikunga and Kashekezi
breakdown.notes: ANC/APR/FAB elements killed more than 800 people between 30 December 1998 and 2 January 1999.
APR and UPDF fighting from 5 to 10 June 2000 killed 244-760 civilians according to Mapping Report sources.

breakdown.cause: one-sided violence attributed to Uganda and affiliates
breakdown.notes: UCDP-linked Uganda/affiliate one-sided civilian deaths were 32+240; ACLED gave 117+170; Uganda's review of the UN Mapping Report found about 2,291 deaths with reasonable suspicion of Ugandan involvement.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The low estimate is a non-exhaustive sum of selected quantified Rwanda/RCD/Burundi/Uganda events; Uganda-specific database estimates are not a full anti-Kabila coalition total.

deaths_from_actions.notes: DRC's ICJ memorial claimed 20,000 additional Ituri deaths attributable to Uganda as occupying power, excluding direct attacks; applying the ICJ expert's model to DRC's proportions produced about 73,994 Ituri excess deaths attributable to Ugandan and allied forces.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Congolese civilians and combatants in DRC
deaths_from_actions.notes: DRC's litigation position attributed 180,000 deaths to Uganda armed action plus 2,000 FAC deaths; the ICJ expert's comparable model produced 224,449 excess deaths. These are contested litigation/model apportionments, not an accepted historical total.
ANC/APR/UPDF troops stopped Inga dam turbines for three weeks in August-September 1998, depriving areas of water and electricity and increasing mortality in medical centres; no death toll is given.

sides.name: Local, communal and proxy armed groups

military_killed.notes: No reliable military-death total by this side was found. In Ituri, UCDP/PRIO recorded 1,036 military deaths and 3,593 unknown-status deaths, but not by group.

breakdown.victims_group: Hema-Gegere and Bira civilians in Nyakunde and surrounding villages
breakdown.notes: FRPI and APC elements massacred over 1,000 civilians between 5 and 15 September 2002.
FRPI elements killed around 150 people on 13 September 2002.
UPC elements supported by Bira militiamen killed at least 14 civilians and burned over 1,000 houses.
UCDP/PRIO recorded 4,341 targeted civilian deaths in Ituri, but this includes multiple perpetrators and cannot be assigned solely to local militias.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The low estimate is a sum of quantified local/proxy militia events. The high indicator is Ituri targeted civilian deaths, not solely local-militia perpetration.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Congolese civilians in Ituri, Kivu, Katanga, Equateur and other affected regions
deaths_from_actions.notes: Local armed groups caused displacement, village burning, looting, road insecurity and collapse of food and health access, but no defensible group-specific indirect-death estimate was found.

military_killed.notes: UCDP/PRIO best estimate records 6,494 armed-actor deaths and 7,824 deaths of unknown civilian/military status. The high is a constructed upper bound if all unknown-status deaths were armed actors.

breakdown.notes: UCDP/PRIO targeted civilian deaths for all DRC, 1 August 1998-2 June 2003.
UCDP/PRIO collateral civilian victims for all DRC, 1 August 1998-2 June 2003.
UCDP/PRIO could not classify 7,824 direct-death victims as civilian or military. High civilian total adds these to known civilian deaths.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Whole-war direct civilian death accounting. Actor attribution is not available from UCDP/PRIO.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Point is ICJ expert model's indirect civilian excess deaths for 1998-2003. Low is constructed from ADRASS 200,000 total conflict-death estimate minus 14,663 known direct civilian deaths. High is constructed from ICJ model 7.1 million upper interval minus 14,663 known direct civilian deaths.

totals.notes: Direct UCDP/PRIO deaths: 28,981 best estimate, 27,817-50,836 low/high, including 14,663 civilians, 6,494 military and 7,824 unknown. Overall conflict-death/excess-mortality range uses the low ADRASS 200,000 estimate cited by ICJ experts and the ICJ expert model interval up to 7.1 million; central scholarly/humanitarian estimates cluster around 3.3-5.0 million for 1998-2003.

key_atrocities.name: Kasika/Kilungutwe/Kalama massacre
key_atrocities.notes: Over 1,000 civilians massacred on 24 August 1998; many women raped, tortured and mutilated before killing.

key_atrocities.name: Makobola II, Bangwe, Katuta, Mikunga and Kashekezi massacres
key_atrocities.victims_group: civilians, including women, children, Red Cross volunteers and religious leaders
key_atrocities.notes: More than 800 people killed between 30 December 1998 and 2 January 1999.

key_atrocities.name: Kinshasa anti-Tutsi and anti-infiltrator killings
key_atrocities.perpetrator: popular defence groups and FAC/security forces
key_atrocities.victims_group: Tutsi, Rwandan-origin civilians and suspected infiltrators
key_atrocities.notes: At least 80 killed in late August 1998; broader campaign included several thousand arrests and several hundred disappearances.

key_atrocities.name: Kisangani Six-Day War
key_atrocities.notes: Rwandan and Ugandan forces fought with heavy weapons in Kisangani from 5 to 10 June 2000; UN-linked figures cite 760 killed, 1,700 wounded, over 4,000 houses damaged/destroyed/uninhabitable, and 65,000 displaced.

key_atrocities.name: Nyakunde massacre
key_atrocities.notes: Over 1,000 civilians killed between 5 and 15 September 2002, selected by ethnic origin.

uncertainty_notes: Direct violent deaths are better documented than indirect deaths, but even direct deaths cannot be reliably assigned by side because UCDP/PRIO did not break down deaths by actor. Excess mortality is highly sensitive to baseline mortality and population assumptions. The largest death category is indirect civilian mortality from disease, malnutrition, displacement and health-system collapse, which the literature generally treats as conflict-wide rather than attributable to one belligerent. The report therefore gives side-specific quantified atrocity examples where possible and keeps the main indirect-death burden in an unattributed conflict-wide category.

sources.title: Democratic Republic of the Congo, 1993–2003: Report of the Mapping Exercise
sources.publisher: United Nations Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights

sources.publisher: University of Southampton ePrints / IUSSP conference paper

sources.title: Ituri: Covered in Blood. Ethnically Targeted Violence in Northeastern DR Congo
Kisangani's Unhealed Scars: The Six-Day War and the ongoing struggle for justice and accountability
